SOURCE 3: ANONYMOUS RN - ADDICTION TO LICENSE REINSTATEMENT (9.5/10)

URL: https://www.myamericannurse.com/my-journey-to-addiction-and-back/
PUBLICATION: American Nurse Journal (October 2018)
AUTHOR: Anonymous (requested due to stigma)
EDITOR'S NOTE: Supplement to CNE program "Suicide among nurses: What we don't know might hurt us"

==================================================================================
FULL ARTICLE TEXT
==================================================================================

[Full article text from document provided - preserved for reference]

Heart, soul, and redemption: My journey to addiction—and back

Editor's note: This blog is a supplement to the continuing nurse education program "Suicide among nurses: What we don't know might hurt us." It illustrates how our unrealistic self-expectations as nurses can lead us down unfortunate paths. The author's identity is not shared per request; unfortunately, there is still stigma associated with substance misuse. We thank the author for the courage to share this personal story and urge nurses to seek the help they need.

[FULL ARTICLE TEXT INCLUDED - See document for complete text]

==================================================================================
NARRATIVE ARC SUMMARY
==================================================================================

ICU Registered Nurse (CCRN, master's prepared, 4 years experience) → brother's sudden death from MI (same day as patient death) → unable to cope with patient deaths → "I saw a mom, a dad, a sister, or a brother. A brother like my brother" → hiding emotions to appear competent → insomnia, night terrors, flashbacks, PTSD → "fear you would feel if you were being buried alive" → Benadryl (150mg, 6x recommended dose) → "About 6 hours of sleep was almost 3 times the sleep I was used to getting" → Vicodin from leftover prescription → "I felt normal, I felt happy. Nostalgia and memories of the past when I was on fire for my life and profession" → friend supplied Norcos for months → became "ultimate consumer of everything gratifying" → friend relocated → full withdrawal at work → "felt like being infected with the H1N1 influenza virus multiplied by ten" → code blue on young MI patient (trigger) → "first thoughts that crossed my mind, for whatever reason, was suicide" → arterial line insertion kit and bathtub plan → "my goal was to end my nightmare, to drain my body of life" → stopped only by thought of 6-year-old son → "not his love for me, but his love for ME" → diverted Fentanyl from hospital (injectable, IM) → caught by staff member → terminated → wife told him he was "addict" (anger, denial) → rehab → 12 felonies filed → local ABC news at front door → "featured 'breaking news' story" → 9 months on disability → relapsed at new job (dialysis clinic with Fentanyl access) → caught again by supervisor → arrested → 10 months in county jail → bunkie sharpening pencils (charged with murder) → "praying to God that he wouldn't slit my throat in my sleep" → referred to as "nurse" by cellmates → talked to everyone → "I was one of them" → "they were all dealing with trauma" → "addiction is merely a symptom of complete spiritual brokenness" → spiritual awakening → "within days I was off the mood stabilizer and antidepressants...truly a miraculous experience" → released → masonry work for year → "transformed from overweight and out-of-shape couch potato into fit construction worker" → wife suggested license reinstatement → "How could it be possible to get my RN license back after all that I had done?" → applied → 1.5 year wait → reinstatement hearing → "voice trembled and tears ran down my face...tears of joy...very similar to the tears and emotion you see in an Olympian when receiving a gold medal" → board members cried → license reinstated with probation → "I was officially and legally allowed to again put an 'RN' after my name" → now practicing nursing again

==================================================================================
KEY EMOTIONAL MOMENTS (25+)
==================================================================================

1. "Brahms Lullaby echoing from labor and delivery as my patient would take his or her last breath"
2. 24-year-old alcoholic patient died + brother's MI same day
3. "I could only feel loss"
4. First day back lasted 20 minutes
5. "I saw a mom, a dad, a sister, or a brother. A brother like my brother"
6. Sobbed uncontrollably, grabbed colleague, surrounded by friends
7. "Making it through the day was a daunting, seemingly impossible task"
8. "Fear you would feel if you were being buried alive"
9. Benadryl: "6 hours of sleep was almost 3 times the sleep I was used to getting. Finally, I found relief"
10. First Vicodin: "I felt normal, I felt happy. Nostalgia and memories of the past when I was on fire for my life"
11. "I couldn't see the harm...I couldn't see the betrayal...I couldn't see the addiction. All I saw was a life free of misery"
12. "I had become a full-blown drug addict: The same guy who excelled in his personal and professional life"
13. "I couldn't do the unthinkable. I couldn't take narcotics from my hospital. That was a line I was not willing to cross."
14. Code blue on young MI patient (trigger)
15. "The first thoughts that crossed my mind, for whatever reason, was suicide"
16. Arterial line insertion kit: "My goal was to end my nightmare, to drain my body of life"
17. Son stopped him: "not his love for me, but his love for ME. I couldn't bear to think of the pain he would go through"
18. First diversion: "In that moment, the morality of right and wrong were nonexistent to me"
19. Wife's devastation: "Not her husband, the husband who was respected by everyone"
20. Wife told him "you are an addict" - anger-filled response
21. SWAT team raided house
22. ABC News at front door with camera: "featured 'breaking news' story"
23. Jail bunkie sharpening pencils: "praying to God that he wouldn't slit my throat in my sleep"
24. "I was referred to as 'nurse' by my cellmates"
25. "I was one of them"
26. "Addiction is merely a symptom of complete spiritual brokenness"
27. "Within days I was off the mood stabilizer and antidepressants...truly a miraculous experience"
28. Wife suggested license reinstatement: "How could it be possible to get my RN license back?"
29. Reinstatement hearing: "voice trembled and tears ran down my face...tears of joy"
30. "Even some in the audience and a board member could not hold back their tears"
31. "My license was reinstated"

==================================================================================
DIRECT QUOTES (PRESERVED FOR STORY)
==================================================================================

BEGINNING - THE FALL:
"I always thought it was quite ironic hearing 'Brahms Lullaby' echoing from labor and delivery as my patient would take his or her last breath."

"I could only feel loss."

"As I walked into the unit to get my assignment, I saw the tragedy in my work. I looked into the rooms, 19 in all, and I saw a mom, a dad, a sister, or a brother. A brother like my brother."

"Making it through the day was a daunting, seemingly impossible task."

"I can only describe it as the fear you would feel if you were being buried alive—a viable threat to your very existence, without the existence of any tangible dangers."

MIDDLE - THE ADDICTION:
"About 6 hours of sleep was almost 3 times the sleep I was used to getting. My problems were solved."

"I felt normal, I felt happy. Nostalgia and memories of the past when I was on fire for my life and profession flooded my brain."

"I couldn't see the harm I was causing my body. I couldn't see the betrayal of my family and colleagues. I couldn't see the addiction. I couldn't see the consequences. All I saw was a life free of misery, a life free of pain."

"I had become a full-blown drug addict: The same guy who excelled in his personal and professional life. The same guy who never felt the bondage with any mind-altering substance for the first 36 years of his life."

CRISIS - THE BOTTOM:
"The best way I could explain it, is that it felt like being infected with the H1N1 influenza virus multiplied by ten."

"The first thoughts that crossed my mind, for whatever reason, was suicide."

"An arterial line insertion kit and my bathtub. That was my plan. I would simply insert the needle, leave it uncapped, and slowly exsanguinate to death in the comfort of a warm bathtub."

"It was my son—the one thing that stopped me, my reason to live. It was not his love for me, but his love for ME. I couldn't bear to think of the pain he would go through knowing his dad would no longer be by his side."

"In that moment, the morality of right and wrong were nonexistent to me."

CONSEQUENCES:
"Not her husband, the husband who was respected by everyone and excelled in his profession."

"I was the featured 'breaking news' story on the local news."

"I'll never forget laying in my bunk at the county jail trying to fall asleep, listening to my 'bunkie' sharping pencils with a small razor blade used for shaving—an inmate being charged with killing a man in broad daylight in front of multiple witnesses. And there I was, praying to God that he wouldn't slit my throat in my sleep."

TRANSFORMATION - THE AWAKENING:
"I was referred to as 'nurse' by my cellmates."

"I was one of them; a man who was decimating his very existence by his inability to deal with life."

"I found one common theme amongst all the inmates. They were all dealing with trauma, negative events and experiences that greatly changed the trajectory of their lives."

"Addiction is a complicating phenomenon. Whether you think it is a moral issue or disease, it is hard to truly grasp the root cause. For me, I found that addiction is merely a symptom of complete spiritual brokenness."

"Within days I was off the mood stabilizer and antidepressants I had been taking to just simply get out of bed every morning. It was truly a miraculous experience."

REDEMPTION - THE RETURN:
"I was transformed from an overweight and out-of-shape couch potato into a fit construction worker."

"How could it be possible to get my RN license back after all that I had done?"

"As I read my statement, my voice trembled and tears ran down my face. I was not emotional over the mistakes I had made, or even the guilt that I felt. What tugged at my heartstrings was the incredible journey and success that I had in recovery, very similar to the tears and emotion you see in an Olympian when receiving a gold medal."

"Even some in the audience and a board member could not hold back their tears."

"My license was reinstated, and I would be fully reinstated once I completed a board-ordered probation. I was officially and legally allowed to again put an 'RN' after my name."

REFLECTION:
"Today, I live with a mended heart and soul. The scars do not show from the outside, but those closest to me know that they are there."

"The purpose of this article is to bring awareness and to give a different perspective to substance abuse and diversion amongst health professionals."

"Change can only happen once the stigma of addiction, and any other behavioral issues, are accepted not as a personal flaw, but as reaction to the immense weight placed on our shoulders as clinicians."

==================================================================================
ESL MULTIPLIERS (7/7) - ALL PRESENT
==================================================================================

✓ BUILDING NEW LIFE
- ICU nurse → addict → incarcerated → masonry worker → practicing RN again
- Lost career, rebuilt from ground up
- Physical transformation (overweight → fit construction worker)
- Professional restoration (license reinstated)
- Family restoration (wife's support)

✓ BELONGING
- Colleagues surrounded him when sobbing (20 minutes back)
- Wife stood by him throughout
- Cellmates called him "nurse" (found purpose in jail)
- Masonry colleagues gave him chance
- Former colleagues supported license reinstatement
- Board members cried at hearing (human connection)

✓ CONNECTION ACROSS DIFFERENCES
- ICU nurse → jail inmates ("I was one of them")
- Master's prepared → masonry worker
- Respected clinician → "low-life drug addict"
- Healthcare professional → construction worker
- Felon → licensed professional

✓ OVERCOMING "NOT GOOD ENOUGH"
- 12 felonies ("low-life drug addict", "threat to the public")
- "Breaking news" story (public shame)
- Stigma of addiction in nursing
- "I did not deserve their love and compassion"
- → License reinstated (board members cried)
- → "Officially and legally allowed to again put an 'RN' after my name"

✓ PERSISTENCE
- Multiple relapses but kept trying
- 10 months in jail used for growth
- Masonry work for year (physical transformation)
- 1.5 year wait for hearing
- Documented all meetings, tracked recovery
- Never gave up on redemption

✓ FIRST-TIME COURAGE
- First time asking for help (couldn't do it initially)
- First time admitting addiction (denial, then acceptance)
- First time sharing story at hearing (voice trembled)
- First time believing redemption possible
- First time returning to nursing

✓ IDENTITY CRISIS (UNIQUE TO THIS SOURCE)
- "I was no longer the respected clinician that I had worked so hard to become"
- "I had become a low-life drug addict"
- "I was one of them"
- "Who am I?" (nurse vs. addict vs. prisoner vs. construction worker)
- Resolution: "I was officially and legally allowed to again put an 'RN' after my name"

==================================================================================
TRANSFORMATION ANALYSIS
==================================================================================

BEFORE:
- Master's prepared CCRN
- 4 years ICU experience
- Educator, university instructor
- Respected clinician
- "Up and coming star within the healthcare community"
- Loving husband and father
- Conservative, planning, responsible

DURING - THE FALL:
- Brother's death (MI, "widow maker")
- Unable to cope with patient deaths
- PTSD, insomnia, night terrors, flashbacks
- Hiding emotions to appear competent
- Benadryl addiction (150mg)
- Vicodin/Norco addiction
- "Ultimate consumer" (cars, food, trips)
- Diversion (Fentanyl from hospital)
- Suicide contemplation (arterial line kit)
- Caught, terminated
- 12 felonies filed
- Local news "breaking story"
- 9 months on disability
- Relapsed at new job
- Caught again
- Arrested

DURING - THE BOTTOM:
- 10 months in county jail
- Bunkie charged with murder
- "Low-life incarcerated drug addict"
- "I was one of them"
- Lost everything: career, liberty, reputation, health
- Marriage in shambles
- Every degree, accolade nullified

DURING - THE AWAKENING:
- Called "nurse" by cellmates
- Talked to inmates, understood trauma
- "Addiction is merely a symptom of complete spiritual brokenness"
- Spiritual transformation
- Off medications within days
- "Truly a miraculous experience"

DURING - THE REBUILDING:
- Released from jail
- Masonry work for year
- Physical transformation (overweight → fit)
- Wife's suggestion (license reinstatement)
- Applied despite improbability
- Tracked recovery for 1.5 years
- Documented everything

AFTER - THE REDEMPTION:
- Reinstatement hearing (tears of joy, Olympic comparison)
- Board members cried
- License reinstated with probation
- "RN" after name again
- Practicing nursing
- Mended heart and soul
- Sharing story publicly (despite stigma)
- Advocating for addiction awareness

==================================================================================
SYSTEMIC IMPACT
==================================================================================

INDIVIDUAL IMPACT:
- 12 felonies → license reinstated (unprecedented)
- Jail → practicing nurse
- "Low-life drug addict" → respected clinician
- Suicidal → purposeful
- Broken → healed

HEALTHCARE IMPACT:
- Challenges stigma of addiction in nursing
- Published in peer-reviewed nursing journal
- "Not unique" - many nurses diverting
- "Culture within nursing profession promotes personal strength and competency, but ignores susceptibility"
- Calls for acceptance of addiction as reaction to "immense weight placed on our shoulders as clinicians"

LICENSING BOARD IMPACT:
- Demonstrates license reinstatement possible
- Board members emotional response (empathy)
- Colleagues supported reinstatement
- Model for redemption in professional licensing

FAMILY IMPACT:
- Son saved his life (literal)
- Wife stood by him (unconditional love)
- "Wife is a model for unconditional love" (comment)
- Family restoration

CRIMINAL JUSTICE IMPACT:
- Jail as transformative experience
- "Most productive 10 months"
- Trauma understanding
- Spiritual awakening in darkest place

==================================================================================
WHY THIS WORKS FOR ESL LEARNERS
==================================================================================

1. UNIVERSAL THEMES:
   - Work stress affects mental health
   - Hiding problems makes them worse
   - Addiction crosses all boundaries
   - Family love saves lives
   - Spiritual transformation heals
   - Second chances exist even after serious mistakes
   - Stigma must be challenged

2. HEALTHCARE WORKER ANGLE:
   - Relatable profession (nurses, doctors, caregivers exist globally)
   - PTSD from work (universal occupational hazard)
   - Helping profession → needing help (irony)
   - Professional licensing (exists in many countries)

3. MENTAL HEALTH EMPHASIS:
   - PTSD clearly explained
   - Grief process detailed
   - Addiction as symptom not cause
   - "Spiritual brokenness" as root
   - Healing journey mapped

4. FAMILY IMPACT:
   - Son as reason to live (parental love)
   - Wife's support (marital commitment)
   - Family restoration (hope)

5. MULTIPLE SECOND CHANCES:
   - Caught → rehab (first chance)
   - Relapsed → caught again (second chance)
   - Jail → spiritual awakening (third chance)
   - Masonry → license application (fourth chance)
   - Hearing → reinstated (fifth chance)
   - Each failure contained seeds of next success

6. CONCRETE MEDICAL DETAILS:
   - Arterial line (specific suicide plan)
   - Fentanyl (specific drug)
   - CCRN, master's (specific credentials)
   - 12 felonies (specific consequences)
   - 10 months (specific time)
   - Creates vivid, memorable imagery

==================================================================================
DIVERSITY CONTRIBUTION
==================================================================================

ADDS TO SECOND CHANCE STORY:
- Professional addiction (different from criminal background)
- Mental health/PTSD trigger (different from poverty/abuse)
- Healthcare field (different from tech/education)
- Shorter incarceration (10 months vs. 16-24 years)
- Middle-class background (different socioeconomic)
- License reinstatement (different redemption path)
- Suicide contemplation (different crisis type)
- Family restoration emphasis (different from individual/community focus)

UNIQUE ELEMENTS:
- Medical suicide plan (arterial line)
- Diversion from healthcare facility
- PTSD from patient deaths
- Brother's death as trigger
- "Breaking news" public exposure
- Jail as educational experience ("I was one of them")
- Physical transformation (construction work)
- License reinstatement hearing (Olympic tears)
- Board members crying
- Spiritual awakening over medication
- Healthcare addiction stigma
- Professional licensing redemption

COMPLEMENTS OTHER SOURCES:
- RN = Healthcare (Brett = Tech, Sean = Education)
- RN = Family restoration (Brett = Individual freedom, Sean = Community building)
- RN = License return (Brett = New career, Sean = Leadership role)
- RN = Mental health (Brett = Foster trauma, Sean = Youth incarceration)
- RN = Short jail (Brett = 60yr sentence, Sean = 24yr sentence)
- RN = Middle class (Brett = Working class, Sean = Urban youth)

==================================================================================
RATING JUSTIFICATION: 9.5/10
==================================================================================

STRENGTHS (+):
- EXCEPTIONAL transformation arc (10/10)
- DEEPLY emotional moments (25+ specific, visceral beats)
- Medical precision in details (arterial line, Fentanyl, CCRN)
- Suicide contemplation with specific plan (powerful crisis)
- Son as sole reason to live (universal parental love)
- "I was one of them" (empathy breakthrough)
- Spiritual transformation (miraculous medication cessation)
- Physical transformation (overweight → fit)
- Professional restoration (license reinstated)
- Board members cried (systemic validation)
- Published despite stigma (advocacy)
- All 7 ESL multipliers (including Identity Crisis)
- Mental health/PTSD angle (crucial diversity)
- Healthcare worker perspective (relatable profession)
- Family impact detailed (son, wife)
- Multiple relapses (realistic, not linear)
- Jail as productive (reframes incarceration)

MINOR WEAKNESSES (-0.5):
- Anonymous author (no name/face, less personal connection)
- Already in healthcare (less dramatic career shift than prison→programmer)

OVERALL:
Extraordinary source. The RN's story adds crucial mental health/PTSD angle that Brett and Sean don't have. The suicide contemplation with medical precision (arterial line) is visceral and memorable. The "I was one of them" breakthrough in jail connects all three sources. The license reinstatement (board members crying, Olympic comparison) is unprecedented and inspiring. The healthcare addiction stigma adds professional dimension. Perfect complement to Brett's tech freedom and Sean's education leadership. The three sources together cover: prison→tech, prison→education, professional→prison→restoration.

==================================================================================
USAGE NOTES FOR STORY CREATION
==================================================================================

KEY MOMENTS TO PRESERVE:
1. "I saw a mom, a dad, a sister, or a brother. A brother like my brother"
2. "Fear you would feel if you were being buried alive" (PTSD description)
3. First Vicodin: "I felt normal, I felt happy"
4. Arterial line kit: "My goal was to end my nightmare"
5. Son: "Not his love for me, but his love for ME"
6. "I was one of them" (jail breakthrough)
7. "Addiction is merely a symptom of complete spiritual brokenness"
8. "Transformed from overweight...to fit construction worker"
9. "How could it be possible to get my RN license back?"
10. Hearing: "Tears of joy...like an Olympian"
11. Board members cried
12. "Officially and legally allowed to again put an 'RN' after my name"

EMOTIONAL BEATS TO HIT:
- Competence → Tragedy → PTSD → Hiding → Addiction → Crisis → Bottom → Awakening → Rebuilding → Redemption

THEMES TO WEAVE:
- Mental health matters
- Asking for help is strength not weakness
- Addiction is symptom not cause
- Family love saves lives
- Spiritual transformation heals
- Professional redemption possible
- Stigma must be challenged
- "I was one of them" (shared humanity)

CONTRAST WITH OTHER SOURCES:
- RN = Mental health/PTSD (Brett = Foster abuse, Sean = Youth crime)
- RN = Healthcare worker (Brett = Programmer, Sean = Educator)
- RN = Family restoration (Brett = Individual freedom, Sean = Community)
- RN = License return (Brett = New career, Sean = Leadership)
- RN = Short jail (Brett = 60 years, Sean = 24 years)
- RN = Middle class (Brett = Working class, Sean = Urban)

==================================================================================
FINAL NOTE
==================================================================================

This source is particularly powerful because:
1. It shows addiction can happen to anyone (respected professional, loving father, 36 years without substance use)
2. It demonstrates mental health crisis leading to addiction (PTSD, not character flaw)
3. It proves professional redemption is possible even after serious criminal charges
4. It challenges healthcare addiction stigma publicly
5. It emphasizes spiritual/community transformation over medication alone
6. It shows family love as lifesaving force
7. It demonstrates that "hitting bottom" can become foundation for new life

The combination of Brett (tech), Sean (education), and RN (healthcare) provides three distinct but complementary paths to redemption, all emphasizing: persistence, community, vulnerability, education/growth, and the possibility of extraordinary transformation even from extraordinary failure.
